revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鼉龍鏡]]


{{Single source|date=March 2023|qq=}}
[[File:柳井茶臼山古墳出土 だ龍鏡 (J-180).JPG|thumb|Excavation from {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|en|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|qid=Q11534089|lt=Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|1=Yanai Chausuyama Kofun}}]]
[[File:菖蒲塚古墳出土 だ龍鏡.JPG|thumb|Excavation from {{ill|Ayamezuka Kofun|en|Ayamezuka Kofun|qid=Q11619951|lt=Ayamezuka Kofun|1=Ayamezuka Kofun}}]]
[[File:雪野山古墳出土 だ龍鏡 (2号鏡).JPG|thumb|Excavation from {{ill|Yukinoyama Kofun|en|Yukinoyama Kofun|qid=Q11659952|lt=Yukinoyama Kofun|1=Yukinoyama Kofun}}]]
'''Taryu Mirror''' ({{lang-ja|鼉龍鏡|qq=}}, {{transl|ja|だりゅうきょう|qq=}}) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. The name derives from researchers interpreting the patterns on the back of the mirror as depicting the taryu (鼉龍), an imaginary beast resembling a {{ill|Crocodilia|en|Crocodilia|qid=Q25363|lt=crocodile|1=Crocodilia}} in {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|qid=Q210677|lt=Chinese mythology|1=Chinese mythology}}. As of 2022, these mirrors are considered {{ill|Wa-produced mirrors|ja|倭製鏡|1=Bōseikyō|lt=Wa-produced mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wa-produced mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōseikyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仿製鏡|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仿製鏡}}, imitations of the {{ill|Han-style mirror|lt=Han-style mirror|lt=Han-style mirrors|ja|漢鏡|1=Han-style mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-style mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-style mirror|qq=}}, specifically the {{ill|Shinju-kyo|en|Shinju-kyo|qid=Q7497614|lt=Mirrors with images of deities and mythical beasts|1=Shinju-kyo}} of the circular band type ({{ill|garibuntai-shinjukyo|ja|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/garibuntai-shinjukyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|pp=71-72|qq=}}{{sfn|Kotobank: 鼉龍鏡|qq=}}
== Overview ==
Approximately 70 taryu mirrors have been confirmed across Japan.{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|p=82|qq=}} These mirrors range from medium to large in size, with larger examples concentrated in the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|qid=Q906708|lt=Kinai region|1=Kinai}} and smaller ones found further away, suggesting that central powers in Kinai produced them and distributed them to local leaders.{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|pp=86-88|qq=}}{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|pp=88-93|qq=}}
== Background ==
{{seealso|Han-style mirrors#History in Japan|qq=}}
The introduction of bronze mirrors to Japan began with the importation of {{ill|Multibossed mirrors|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}, followed by the influx of {{ill|Han-style mirror|lt=Han-style mirror|lt=Han-style mirrors|ja|漢鏡|1=Han-style mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-style mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-style mirror|qq=}} during the latter half of the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Middle Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}}.{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|pp=26-28|qq=}} Han-style mirrors were highly valued as {{ill|prestige goods|ja|威信財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prestige goods|qq=}}, but their availability was often subject to the political circumstances of surrounding states, including the Han Dynasty. As demand outpaced supply, domestic production of Wa mirrors began.{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|pp=28-31|qq=}}
While the {{ill|Han-style mirror|lt=Han-style mirror|lt=Han-style mirrors|ja|漢鏡|1=Han-style mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-style mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-style mirror|qq=}}, particularly those with images of deities and mythical beasts, were admired, the systematic acceptance of Chinese Taoist ideologies they depicted was limited in Japan. Craftsmen in Japan emphasized the imagery but often simplified or transformed it, as they did not fully understand the underlying ideologies. Taryu mirrors, modeled on circular-band deity-beast mirrors, exemplify this phenomenon.{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|pp=71-72|qq=}}
The original Chinese deity-beast mirrors depicted long-necked mythical beasts holding a staff in their mouths, with a deity riding on their backs. Initially, the imagery of taryu mirrors adhered closely to the Chinese prototypes, but over time, the deity's and beast's heads were merged into one, and the bodies of both were depicted in a two-bodied single-head style.{{sfn|Shitagaki Hitoshi|2022|pp=71-72|qq=}}
== Notes ==
{{Notes-help|qq=}}
=== References ===
{{Reflist|3|qq=}}
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Shitagaki Hitoshi|authorlink=下垣仁志|title=The Kofun Period of Mirrors (鏡の古墳時代)|year=2022|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}}|series=Historical Culture Library 547 (歴史文化ライブラリー 547)|isbn=9784642059473|ref=harv|qq=}}
* “{{ill|Kotobank|en|Kotobank|qid=Q11302260|lt=Kotobank|1=Kotobank}}”. {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|1=Asahi Shimbun Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q444649}}, {{ill|CARTA Holdings|ja|CARTA HOLDINGS|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/CARTA Holdings|1=Carta Holdings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11252820}}.
''' {{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BC%89%E7%AB%9C%E9%8F%A1-1183923|title=Taryu Mirror (鼉竜鏡)|accessdate=2023-03-16|ref={{sfnref|Kotobank: 鼉竜鏡|qq=}}|qq=}} (Excerpted from {{ill|Sekai Daijiten|ja|世界大百科事典 第２版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekai Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
